summary: Jeff Atwood endorses Jon Udell's argument that most keystrokes spent on private emails could have far greater impact as public blog posts. The post argues that blogging optimizes for maximum awareness and influence per keystroke, since the same information that helps three email recipients could benefit many more readers. Atwood describes his own habit of converting emails into blog posts when he realizes the information shouldn't be siloed. The core message is that making communication public by default creates powerful network effects with minimal extra effort.
key_points: Most keystrokes go into emails read by few people, when many more could benefit from that same information
Blogging optimizes the awareness and influence each keystroke can yield
Converting private emails to public blog posts costs almost no extra effort but radically expands reach
The notification of a message and its delivery don't have to coincide - you can notify privately while making the payload public
Default to public communication unless the topic is necessarily private and interpersonal
money_quotes: How many of those keystrokes flow into email messages? Most. How many people receive those email messages? Few. How many people could usefully benefit from those messages, now or later? More than a few, maybe a lot more.
Why not make each keystroke work as hard as it can?
That arrangement costs hardly any extra keystrokes, and hardly any extra time. But it's an optimization that can radically expand influence and awareness.
There's no reason to silo this information in a private email exchange.
The next time you find yourself typing more than a few sentences on your keyboard, stop and ask: are you maximizing the value of your keystrokes?
key_insight: Every substantial piece of writing you create privately is a missed opportunity for broader impact - default to public sharing unless privacy is truly required.
